cards.back: Python's standard library for building graphical user interfaces (GUIs). Provides widgets like buttons, labels, and entry fields for creating desktop applications.
Creates the main application window. First step in any Tkinter program. Example: window = tk.Tk()
A visual element in a GUI like Label, Button, Entry, Text, Frame. Each widget has properties (text, color) and can respond to events.
Displays text or images on the window. Non-interactive. Example: tk.Label(window, text='Name:')
A clickable button. Connected to a function via the command parameter. Example: tk.Button(window, text='Click', command=on_click)
Single-line text input field. Read value with .get(). Example: name = entry.get()
Stacks widgets vertically or horizontally. Simple layout manager. Example: button.pack()
Arranges widgets in rows and columns like a table. More control than pack(). Example: button.grid(row=0, column=1)
A Tkinter variable that binds to Entry widgets. Changes to StringVar automatically update the widget, and vice versa.
A function connected to a button or other widget. Called when the widget is activated. Example: button.config(command=my_function)
A Tkinter module for showing dialog boxes. Examples: messagebox.showinfo(), messagebox.showerror(), messagebox.askyesno()
Starts the event loop that listens for user interactions. Keeps the window open. Must be called at the end of every Tkinter program.
